g.data-narration: Welcome to Viewing with a Computer - a fundamental concept in computer graphics.
Viewing transforms 3D scene descriptions into what you see on screen through mathematical pipeline stages.
g.text: Systematic process to transform scene descriptions into screen display
Enables camera positioning at any location and direction

g.data-narration: The viewing pipeline has four main stages: Modeling, Viewing, Projection, and Viewport transformations.
Modeling transformation positions objects in the world using translation, rotation, and scaling operations.
Viewing transformation establishes a camera coordinate system with origin at the camera position.
Projection transforms 3D to 2D, then viewport maps to the physical screen display area.
Camera parameters define the viewing coordinate system: VRP, VPN, VUP, and PRP.
Window defines visible world region; viewport maps it to screen while maintaining proportions.
g.text: Window: Rectangular region in world coordinates defining visible scene
Viewport: Rectangular region on screen for display

g.data-narration: The transformation uses scaling factors and offsets to map coordinates proportionally between window and viewport.
Let's work through an example: mapping point P(15,25) from window (0,0)-(100,100) to viewport (100,100)-(400,300).
Clipping removes objects outside the viewing region, optimizing rendering by eliminating invisible geometry.
g.text: Removes portions of objects outside the viewing region (window)
Optimizes rendering by eliminating invisible geometry

g.data-narration: Cohen-Sutherland uses 4-bit outcodes for efficient line clipping: TOP, BOTTOM, RIGHT, LEFT bits.
For line A(10,10) to B(90,90) with window (20,20)-(80,80): outcodes are 1010 and 0101, requiring clipping.
Two projection types: Parallel maintains parallel lines (technical drawings), Perspective creates depth with vanishing points.
Perspective projection uses similar triangles: x' = x × (d/z') where d is distance to projection plane.
Remember: Window-Viewport mapping formula, Cohen-Sutherland outcodes, and perspective projection equations.
You can now explain the viewing pipeline, apply window-viewport mapping, implement clipping, and distinguish projection types.

g.text: Window to Viewport transformation with scaling and translation

g.data-narration: Avoid these mistakes: confusing pipeline order, forgetting trivial acceptance check, and not maintaining aspect ratio.

g.text: Confusing transformation order in viewing pipeline
Not maintaining aspect ratio in window-viewport mapping
